subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math><mi>S</mi></math> be the set of all triangles in the <math><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi></math> -plane, each having one vertex at the origin and the other two vertices lie on coordinate axes with integral coordinates. If each triangle in <math><mi>S</mi></math> has area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> sq. units, then the number of elements in the set <math><mi>S</mi></math> is:
question: Let <math><mi>S</mi></math> be the set of all triangles in the <math><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi></math> -plane, each having one vertex at the origin and the other two vertices lie on coordinate axes with integral coordinates. If each triangle in <math><mi>S</mi></math> has area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> sq. units, then the number of elements in the set <math><mi>S</mi></math> is:
solution:
Since the vertex of given triangle is O (origin) and other two vertices A and B lies on the coordinate axes, hence triangle must be a right-angled triangle.

Area =12x.y                        ( x, y∈I)

As per given condition

12x.y=50⇒x. y=100

Possible ordered pairs of x, y when ∆ formed in 1st quadrant are 9 i. e. 1,100, 2,50, 4,25, 5,20, 10,10, 25, 4, 50, 2, 100,1

∆ can be formed in any of the quadrants, hence total number of cases 9×4=36.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-y=x^{2} \cot x, x \in(0, \pi)$. If $y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=\frac{\pi}{2}$, then $6 y\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)-8 y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ is equal to :
question: Let $y=y(x)$ be the solution of the differential equation $x \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}-y=x^{2} \cot x, x \in(0, \pi)$. If $y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=\frac{\pi}{2}$, then $6 y\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)-8 y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ is equal to :
solution:
The differential equation $x\frac{dy}{dx} - y = x^2 \cot x$ can be rewritten as $\frac{dy}{dx} - \frac{y}{x} = x \cot x$.
The integrating factor is $\mu(x) = \frac{1}{x}$. Multiplying by $\mu(x)$ gives $\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right) = \cot x$.
Integrating: $\frac{y}{x} = \ln|\sin x| + C$, so $y = x\ln(\sin x) + Cx$.
Using $y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) = \frac{\pi}{2}$: we get $\frac{\pi}{2} = 0 + C\frac{\pi}{2}$, thus $C = 1$.
Therefore $y = x(1 + \ln(\sin x))$.
Now: $y\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = \frac{\pi}{6}(1 - \ln 2)$ and $y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(1 - \frac{\ln 2}{2}\right)$.
Thus $6y\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) - 8y\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \pi(1 - \ln 2) - 2\pi\left(1 - \frac{\ln 2}{2}\right) = -\pi$.

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of 6-letter words, with or without meaning, that can be formed using the letters of the word MATHS such that any letter that appears in the word must appear at least twice, is _______.
question: The number of 6-letter words, with or without meaning, that can be formed using the letters of the word MATHS such that any letter that appears in the word must appear at least twice, is _______.
solution:
(i) Single letter is used, then no. of words $=5$
(ii) Two distinct letters are used, then no. of words
${ }^5 \mathrm{C}_2 \times\left(\frac{6!}{2!4!} \times 2+\frac{6!}{3!3!}\right)=10(30+20)=500$
(iii) Three distinct letters are used, then no. of words
${ }^5 \mathrm{C}_3 \times \frac{6!}{2!2!2!}=900$

Total no. of words $=1405$

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></math> be two roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced><mn>20</mn></mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced><mn>5</mn></mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>8</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>β</mi><mn>8</mn></msup></math> is equal to
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></math> be two roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced><mn>20</mn></mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced><mn>5</mn></mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>/</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>8</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>β</mi><mn>8</mn></msup></math> is equal to
solution:
Given, 

x2+201/4x+51/2=0

⇒x2+5=-x2014

⇒x2+52=20x2

⇒x4+5+2×x2×5=20x2

⇒x4=-5⇒x8=25

So, α8+β8=25+25=50

subject: Mathematics
question: If $\mathrm{I}_1=\int_0^1 2^{x^2} d x, I_2=\int_0^1 2^{x^3} d x, I_3=\int_1^2 2^{x^2} d x$ and $I_4=\int_1^2 2^{x^3} d x$ then
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& l_1=\int_0^1 2^{x^2} d x, l_2=\int_0^1 2^{x^3} d x, I_3=\int_0^1 2^{x^2} d x, I_4=\int_0^1 2^{x^3} d x \\
& \forall 0 < x < 1, x^2>x^3 \\
& \Rightarrow \int_0^1 2^{x^2} d x>\int_0^1 2^{x^3} d x \\
& \Rightarrow I_1>I_2 .
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: An ellipse has $\mathrm{OB}$ as semi minor axis, $\mathrm{F}$ and $\mathrm{F}^{\prime}$ its focii and the angle FBF' is a right angle. Then the eccentricity of the ellipse is
question: An ellipse has $\mathrm{OB}$ as semi minor axis, $\mathrm{F}$ and $\mathrm{F}^{\prime}$ its focii and the angle FBF' is a right angle. Then the eccentricity of the ellipse is
solution: $$ \begin{aligned} & \because \angle \text { FBF }^{\prime}=90^{\circ} \\ & \therefore\left(\sqrt{a^2 e^2+b^2}\right)^2+\left(\sqrt{a^2 e^2+b^2}\right)^2=(2 a \mathrm{e})^2 \\ & \Rightarrow 2\left(a^2 e^2+b^2\right)=4 a^2 e^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^2=\mathrm{b}^2 / \mathrm{a}^2 \\ & \text { Also } \mathrm{e}^2=1-\mathrm{b}^2 / \mathrm{a}^2=1-\mathrm{e}^2 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{e}^2=1, \mathrm{e}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} . \end{aligned} $$

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the system of linear equations

x-2y+z=-4

2x+αy+3z=5

3x-y+βz=3

has infinitely many solutions, then 12α+13β is equal to
question:
If the system of linear equations

x-2y+z=-4

2x+αy+3z=5

3x-y+βz=3

has infinitely many solutions, then 12α+13β is equal to
solution:
Given:

The system of linear equations 

x-2y+z=-4, 

2x+αy+3z=5 and

3x-y+βz=3 has infinity many solutions,

So, △=△1=△2=△3

Now, finding, 

△2=1-4125333β=0

⇒5β-9+8β-36-9=0

⇒13β=54

And, △3=1-2-42α53-13=0

⇒3α+5+12-30+8+12α=0

⇒15α=5

⇒12α=4

Hence, the value of 12α+13β=4+54=58

q: From the top A of a vertical wall AB of height 30 m, the angles of depression of the top P and bottom Q of a vertical tower PQ are 15∘ and 60∘ respectively, B and Q are on the same horizontal level. If C is a point on AB such that CB=PQ, then the area (in m2) of the quadrilateral BCPQ is equal to
question: From the top A of a vertical wall AB of height 30 m, the angles of depression of the top P and bottom Q of a vertical tower PQ are 15∘ and 60∘ respectively, B and Q are on the same horizontal level. If C is a point on AB such that CB=PQ, then the area (in m2) of the quadrilateral BCPQ is equal to
solution:
Given that from the top A of a vertical wall AB of height 30 m, the angles of depression of the top P and bottom Q of a vertical tower PQ are 15∘ and 60∘ respectively,

B and Q are on the same horizontal level andC is a point on AB such that CB=PQ.

The required diagram will be

From the diagram,

⇒tan60°=ABBQ

⇒30x=3;

⇒x=103 m

Also, tan15°=ACCP

⇒30-hx=2-3

⇒30-h=1032-3

⇒30-h=203-30

⇒h=60-203

Now area of the quadrilateral BCPQ is BC×BQ

∴ area=hx

=(60-203)103

=20033-3

=6003-1 m2

Hence, this is the correct option.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the line $x+y=1$ meet the axes of $x$ and $y$ at A and B, respectively. A right angled triangle AMN is inscribed in the triangle OAB , where O is the origin and the points M and N lie on the lines $O B$ and $A B$, respectively. If the area of the triangle $A M N$ is $\frac{4}{9}$ of the area of the triangle $O A B$ and AN : NB $=\lambda: 1$, then the sum of all possible value(s) of is $\lambda$ :
question: Let the line $x+y=1$ meet the axes of $x$ and $y$ at A and B, respectively. A right angled triangle AMN is inscribed in the triangle OAB , where O is the origin and the points M and N lie on the lines $O B$ and $A B$, respectively. If the area of the triangle $A M N$ is $\frac{4}{9}$ of the area of the triangle $O A B$ and AN : NB $=\lambda: 1$, then the sum of all possible value(s) of is $\lambda$ :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Area of } \triangle \mathrm{AOB}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \text { Area of } \triangle \mathrm{AMN}=\frac{4}{9} \times \frac{1}{2}=\frac{2}{9} \end{aligned}$ Equation of AB is $\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}=1$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{OA}=1, \mathrm{AM}=\sec \left(45^{\circ}-\theta\right) \\ & \mathrm{AN}=\sec \left(45^{\circ}-\theta\right) \cos \theta \\ & \mathrm{MN}=\sec \left(45^{\circ}-\theta\right) \sin \theta \end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \operatorname{Ar}(\triangle \mathrm{AMN})=\frac{1}{2} \times \sec ^2\left(45^{\circ}-\theta\right) \sin \theta \cdot \cos \theta=\frac{2}{9} \\ & \Rightarrow \tan \theta=2, \frac{1}{2} \\ & \tan \theta=2 \text { is rejected } \\ & \frac{\mathrm{AN}}{\mathrm{NB}}=\frac{\lambda}{1}=\cot \theta=2\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q:
The area (in sq. units) of the region
$\left\{(x, y): 0 \leq \mathrm{y} \leq 2|x|+1,0 \leq \mathrm{y} \leq x^2+1,|x| \leq 3\right\}$ is
question:
The area (in sq. units) of the region
$\left\{(x, y): 0 \leq \mathrm{y} \leq 2|x|+1,0 \leq \mathrm{y} \leq x^2+1,|x| \leq 3\right\}$ is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Area }=2\left[\int_0^2\left(x^2+1\right) d x+\frac{1}{2}[5+7] \times 1\right] \\ & =\frac{64}{3}\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: The value of $k \in \mathrm{N}$ for which the integral $I_n=\int_0^1\left(1-x^k\right)^n d x, n \in \mathbb{N}$, satisfies $147 I_{20}=148 I_{21}$ is
question: The value of $k \in \mathrm{N}$ for which the integral $I_n=\int_0^1\left(1-x^k\right)^n d x, n \in \mathbb{N}$, satisfies $147 I_{20}=148 I_{21}$ is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & I_n=\int_0^1\left(1-x^k\right)^n \cdot 1 d x \\ & I_n=\left(1-x^k\right)^n \cdot x-n k \int_0^1\left(1-x^k\right)^{n-1} \cdot x^{k-1} \cdot d x \\ & I_n=n k \int_0^1\left[\left(1-x^k\right)^n-\left(1-x^k\right)^{n-1}\right] d x \\ & I_n=n k I_n-n k I_n \\ & \frac{I_n}{I_{n-1}}=\frac{n k}{n k+1} \\ & \frac{I_{21}}{I_{20}}=\frac{21 k}{1+21 k} \\ & =\frac{147}{148} \Rightarrow k=7\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let, the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> be defined as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msubsup><mo>∫</mo><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></msubsup><mfrac><msup><mi>e</mi><mi>t</mi></msup><mi>t</mi></mfrac><mi>d</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then the value of the integral <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mo>∫</mo><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></msubsup><mfrac><msup><mi>e</mi><mi>t</mi></msup><mrow><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>a</mi></mrow></mfrac><mi>d</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> is
question: Let, the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> be defined as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msubsup><mo>∫</mo><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></msubsup><mfrac><msup><mi>e</mi><mi>t</mi></msup><mi>t</mi></mfrac><mi>d</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then the value of the integral <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mo>∫</mo><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></msubsup><mfrac><msup><mi>e</mi><mi>t</mi></msup><mrow><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>a</mi></mrow></mfrac><mi>d</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo></math> is
solution:
Given Fx=∫1xettdt

Let, I=∫1xett+adt

Let, t+a=y   ⇒dt=dy

Also, t=1 ⇒y=1+a and t=x ⇒y=x+a

∴  I=∫1+ax+aey-aydy

⇒I=e-a∫1+ax+aeyydy

Using ∫abfxdx=∫abftdt, we get

I=e-a∫1+ax+aettdt

⇒I=e-a∫11+aettdt+∫1+ax+aettdt-∫11+aettdt

Using ∫abfxdx+∫bcfxdx=∫acfxdx, a<c<b,

⇒I=e-a∫1x+aettdt-∫11+aettdt

Using, the given relation, we get

I=e-aFx+a-F1+a.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $\mathrm{ABC}$ be a triangle with vertices at points $\mathrm{A}$ $(2,3,5)$, B $(-1,3,2)$ and $\mathrm{C}(\lambda, 5, \mu)$ in three dimensional space. If the median through $\mathrm{A}$ is equally inclined with the axes, then $(\lambda, \mu)$ is equal to:
question: Let $\mathrm{ABC}$ be a triangle with vertices at points $\mathrm{A}$ $(2,3,5)$, B $(-1,3,2)$ and $\mathrm{C}(\lambda, 5, \mu)$ in three dimensional space. If the median through $\mathrm{A}$ is equally inclined with the axes, then $(\lambda, \mu)$ is equal to:
solution:
Since $\mathrm{AD}$ is the median
$$ \therefore \quad \mathrm{D}=\left(\frac{\lambda-1}{2}, 4, \frac{\mu+2}{2}\right) $$ Now, dR's of AD is $$ a=\left(\frac{\lambda-1}{2}-2\right)=\frac{\lambda-5}{2} $$ $$ b=4-3=1, c=\frac{\mu+2}{2}-5=\frac{\mu-8}{2} $$ Also, $a, b, c$ are $\mathrm{dR}$ 's $\therefore a=k l, b=k m, c=k n$ where $l=m=n$ and $l^2+m^2+n^2=1$ $$ \Rightarrow l=m=n=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} $$ Now, $a=1, b=1$ and $c=1$ $\Rightarrow \lambda=7$ and $\mu=10$

subject: Mathematics
q: A water tank has the shape of an inverted right circular cone, whose semi-vertical angle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> Water is poured into it at a constant rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi><mi>u</mi><mi>b</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>c</mi><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>m</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then the rate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo></math>in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>m</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo></math> at which the level of water is rising at the instant when the depth of water in the tank is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mo>;</mo></math> is:
question: A water tank has the shape of an inverted right circular cone, whose semi-vertical angle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> Water is poured into it at a constant rate of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi><mi>u</mi><mi>b</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>c</mi><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>m</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Then the rate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo></math>in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>m</mi><mi>i</mi><mi>n</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo></math> at which the level of water is rising at the instant when the depth of water in the tank is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi>m</mi><mo>;</mo></math> is:
solution:
The given water tank is of the shape shown by the diagram.

The semi-vertical angle θ=tan-112,  ⇒tanθ=12

Let at any time t min, height of water level is h m  and radius of cone filled with water be r m.

Also, we have tanθ=rh

⇒rh=12

⇒r=h2   ...i

Now, the volume of the water at time t min in the cone is V=13πr2h

On putting the value of r from the equation i, we get

V=π3h34=π12h3

Now, differentiating with respect to t, we get

dVdt=π12⋅3h2dhdt

Put the given value of dVdt & h

⇒5=π4⋅100dhdt

⇒dhdt=15π m/min

subject: Mathematics
q: The set of values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> for which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munder><mi>lim</mi><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>a</mi></mrow></munder><mfenced><mrow><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, where, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi>ζ</mi></mfenced></math> denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ζ</mi></math> is equal to
question: The set of values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> for which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munder><mi>lim</mi><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>a</mi></mrow></munder><mfenced><mrow><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, where, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mi>ζ</mi></mfenced></math> denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ζ</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
We have,

limx→ax-5-2x+2=0

⇒limx→ax-5-2x-2=0

⇒limx→ax-2x-7=0

⇒limx→ax-2x=7

Now when a∈I (integer) then a-2a=7⇒a=-7

Now when a∈I+f where f is fraction,

So, I+f-2I+2f=7

⇒I+f-2I-2f=7

⇒I+0-2I-2f=7

⇒-I-2f=7

Now taking case 1 when f∈0,12

Then ⇒-I-0=7⇒I=-7⇒a∈-7,-6.5

Now taking case 2 when f∈12,1

So, -I-1=7⇒I=-8⇒a∈-7.5,7

Hence, from both the cases we can say that a∈-7.5,-6.5.

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the system of equations
$x + 5y + 6z = 4$,
$2x + 3y + 4z = 7$,
$x + 6y + az = b$
has infinitely many solutions, then the point $(a, b)$ lies on the line
question:
If the system of equations
$x + 5y + 6z = 4$,
$2x + 3y + 4z = 7$,
$x + 6y + az = b$
has infinitely many solutions, then the point $(a, b)$ lies on the line
solution:
For the given system of equations to have infinitely many solutions, the determinant of the coefficient matrix must be zero, $\Delta = 0$.

$\Delta = \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 5 & 6 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 1 & 6 & a \end{vmatrix} = 0$

Expanding along the first row:

$1(3a - 24) - 5(2a - 4) + 6(12 - 3) = 0$

$3a - 24 - 10a + 20 + 54 = 0$

$-7a + 50 = 0 \Rightarrow a = \dfrac{50}{7}$

For infinitely many solutions, we must also have $\Delta_z = 0$.

$\Delta_z = \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 5 & 4 \\ 2 & 3 & 7 \\ 1 & 6 & b \end{vmatrix} = 0$

Expanding along the first row:

$1(3b - 42) - 5(2b - 7) + 4(12 - 3) = 0$

$3b - 42 - 10b + 35 + 36 = 0$

$-7b + 29 = 0 \Rightarrow b = \dfrac{29}{7}$

The point $(a, b)$ is $\left(\dfrac{50}{7}, \dfrac{29}{7}\right)$.

Checking the given options, we evaluate $a - b$:

$a - b = \dfrac{50}{7} - \dfrac{29}{7} = \dfrac{21}{7} = 3$

Therefore, the point $(a, b)$ lies on the line $x - y = 3$.

Answer: $x - y = 3$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let y=y1x and y=y2x be the solution curves the differential equation dydx=y+7 with initial conditions y10=0 and y20=1 respectively. Then the curves y=y1x and y = y2x intersect at
question: Let y=y1x and y=y2x be the solution curves the differential equation dydx=y+7 with initial conditions y10=0 and y20=1 respectively. Then the curves y=y1x and y = y2x intersect at
solution:
We have been given thatdydx=y+7⇒dyy+7=dx

⇒ln|y+7|=x+c

⇒|y+7|=k.ex

⇒y=k·ex-7

⇒y10=0⇒k=7⇒y1x=7ex-1

⇒y20=1⇒1=k-7⇒8=k

⇒y2x=8ex-7

⇒y1x=y2x⇒8ex-7=7ex-7

Hence, No point of intersection.

This is the required option.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Consider the system of linear equations in $x, y, z$:
$x + 2y + tz = 0$,
$6x + y + 5tz = 0$,
$3x + t^2 y + f(t) z = 0$,
where $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is a differentiable function. If this system has infinitely many solutions for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$, then $f$
question:
Consider the system of linear equations in $x, y, z$:
$x + 2y + tz = 0$,
$6x + y + 5tz = 0$,
$3x + t^2 y + f(t) z = 0$,
where $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is a differentiable function. If this system has infinitely many solutions for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$, then $f$
solution:
For a homogeneous system of linear equations to have infinitely many solutions, the determinant of its coefficient matrix must be zero.

The coefficient matrix is:
$\Delta = \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 & t \\ 6 & 1 & 5t \\ 3 & t^2 & f(t) \end{vmatrix}$

Expanding the determinant along the first row, we get:
$\Delta = 1(1 \cdot f(t) - 5t \cdot t^2) - 2(6 \cdot f(t) - 3 \cdot 5t) + t(6 \cdot t^2 - 3 \cdot 1)$

$\Delta = f(t) - 5t^3 - 12f(t) + 30t + 6t^3 - 3t$

$\Delta = -11f(t) + t^3 + 27t$

Since the system has infinitely many solutions for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$, we must have $\Delta = 0$ for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$.

$-11f(t) + t^3 + 27t = 0$

$f(t) = \dfrac{t^3 + 27t}{11}$

To determine the nature of the function $f(t)$, we find its derivative with respect to $t$:

$f'(t) = \dfrac{3t^2 + 27}{11}$

Since $t^2 \ge 0$ for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$, we have $3t^2 + 27 \ge 27 > 0$.

Therefore, $f'(t) > 0$ for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$, which implies that $f(t)$ is a strictly increasing function on $\mathbb{R}$.

Answer: is strictly increasing on $\mathbb{R}$

subject: Mathematics
q:
Consider the function :
$f(x)=[x]+|1-x|,-1 \leq x \leq 3$ where $[x]$ is the greatest integer function.
Statement 1: $f$ is not continuous at $x=0,1,2$ and 3 
Statement 2:f(x)= $=\begin{array}{cc}-x, & -1 \leq x < 0 \\ 1-x, & 0 \leq x < 1 \\ 1+x, & 1 \leq x < 2 \\ 2+x, & 2 \leq x \leq 3\end{array}$
question:
Consider the function :
$f(x)=[x]+|1-x|,-1 \leq x \leq 3$ where $[x]$ is the greatest integer function.
Statement 1: $f$ is not continuous at $x=0,1,2$ and 3 
Statement 2:f(x)= $=\begin{array}{cc}-x, & -1 \leq x < 0 \\ 1-x, & 0 \leq x < 1 \\ 1+x, & 1 \leq x < 2 \\ 2+x, & 2 \leq x \leq 3\end{array}$
options: Statement 1 is true; Statement 2 is false,
Statement 1 is true; Statement 2 is true; Statement 2 is not correct explanation for Statement 1.
Statement 1 is true; Statement 2 is true; Statement It is a correct explanation for Statement 1.
Statement 1 is false; Statement 2 is true.
solution:
Let $f(x)=[x]+|1-x|,-1 \leq x \leq 3$ where $[x]=$ greatest integer function. $f$ is not continuous at $x=0,1,2,3$ But in statement-2 $f(x)$ is continuous at $x=3$.

Hence, statement-1 is true and 2 is false.

q: Let the vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><msub><mi>u</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>a</mi><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mover><msub><mi>u</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><msub><mi>u</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mi>c</mi><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo></math> be coplanar. If the vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mover><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></mrow></mfenced><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>a</mi><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi>v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>b</mi><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>c</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>a</mi></mrow></mfenced><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> are also coplanar, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: Let the vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><msub><mi>u</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>a</mi><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mover><msub><mi>u</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><msub><mi>u</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mi>c</mi><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo></math> be coplanar. If the vectors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mover><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></mrow></mfenced><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>a</mi><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi>v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>b</mi><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>c</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>a</mi></mrow></mfenced><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> are also coplanar, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
Given: u→1=i^+j^+ak^, u→2=i^+bj^+k^ and u→3=ci^+j^+k^ are coplanar.

Also given that,v→1=a+bi^+cj^+ck^, v→2=ai^+b+cj^+ak^and v→3=bi^+bj^+c+ak^ are coplanar.

Now, using the condition of coplanar we get,

⇒11a1b1c11=0

Expanding the determinant along R1.

⇒b-1-1-c+a1-bc=0

⇒a+b+c=2+abc     .....i

Again using the coplanar condition we get,

⇒a+bccab+cabbc+a=0

Apply row transformations, R3→R3-R1+R2

⇒a+bccab+ca-2a-2c0=0

Expand the determinant along R1.

⇒a+b0+2ac-c0+2a2+c-2ac+2ab+c=0

⇒2a2c+2abc-2a2c-2ac2+2abc+2ac2=0

⇒abc=0

∴ a+b+c=2 (From eq i)

∴ 6a+b+c=12

Hence this is the correct option.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>4</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>8</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>16</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mo>"</mo></msup></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is equal to
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>4</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>8</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>16</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mo>"</mo></msup></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is equal to
solution:
Given,

Function yx=1+x1+x21+x41+x81+x16

Now multiplying and dividing by 1-x both side we get,

1-xyx=1-x1+x1+x21+x41+x81+x16

⇒y1-x=1-x32 

⇒y-xy=1-x32 ......(1)

Now differentiating equation 1 on both sides with respect to x we get,

y'-xy'-y=-32x31.......(2)

Now at x=-1

⇒y'(-1)=16

Now differentiating equation 2 with respect to x on both sides we get,

⇒y"-xy"-y'-y'=-3231x30

Now at x=-1

⇒y''(-1)=-480

So, at x=-1, y'-y"=16-(-480)=496

q: A spherical iron ball of <math><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> radius is coated with a layer of ice of uniform thickness that melts at a rate of <math><mn>50</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow></mrow></math> . When the thickness of ice is <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> , then the rate (in <math><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow></mrow></math> .) at which of the thickness of ice decreases, is:
question: A spherical iron ball of <math><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> radius is coated with a layer of ice of uniform thickness that melts at a rate of <math><mn>50</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow></mrow></math> . When the thickness of ice is <math><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> , then the rate (in <math><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow></mrow></math> .) at which of the thickness of ice decreases, is:
solution:
Let thickness =x cm
Total volume V=43π10+x3
dVdt=4π10+x2dxdt…..(i)
Given dVdt=50 cm3/min
At x=5 cm
50=4π10+52dxdt
dxdt=118π cm/min

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $A=\left(\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 2 q & r \\ p & q & -r \\ p & -q & r\end{array}\right)$. If $\mathrm{AA}^{\mathrm{T}}=\mathrm{I}_{3},$ then $|\mathrm{p}|$ is:
question: Let $A=\left(\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 2 q & r \\ p & q & -r \\ p & -q & r\end{array}\right)$. If $\mathrm{AA}^{\mathrm{T}}=\mathrm{I}_{3},$ then $|\mathrm{p}|$ is:
solution:
$A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 2 q & r \\ p & q & -r \\ p & -q & r\end{array}\right]$

$\begin{aligned} \therefore & A \cdot A^{T}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}0 & 2 q & r \\ p & q & -r \\ p & -q & r\end{array}\right] \times\left[\begin{array}{ccc}0 & p & p \\ 2 q & q & -q \\ r & -r & r\end{array}\right] \\ &=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}4 q^{2}+r^{2} & 2 q^{2}-r^{2} & -2 q^{2}+r^{2} \\ 2 q^{2}-r^{2} & p^{2}+q^{2}+r^{2} & p^{2}-q^{2}-r^{2} \\ -2 q^{2}+r^{2} & p^{2}-q^{2}-r^{2} & p^{2}+q^{2}+r^{2}\end{array}\right] \end{aligned}$

Given, $A A^{T}=I$

$\therefore \quad 4 q^{2}+r^{2}=p^{2}+q^{2}+r^{2}=1$

$\Rightarrow \quad p^{2}-3 q^{2}=0$ and $r^{2}=1-4 q^{2}$

and $2 q^{2}-r^{2}=0 \Rightarrow r^{2}=2 q^{2}$

$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore \quad & p^{2}=\frac{1}{2}, q^{2}=\frac{1}{6} \text { and } r^{2}=\frac{1}{3} \\ \therefore \quad & |p|=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\end{array}$

subject: Mathematics
q: A line with direction cosines proportional to $2,1,2$ meets each of the lines $x=y+a=z$ and $x+a=2 y=2 z$. The co-ordinates of each of the point of intersection are given by
question: A line with direction cosines proportional to $2,1,2$ meets each of the lines $x=y+a=z$ and $x+a=2 y=2 z$. The co-ordinates of each of the point of intersection are given by
solution:
Any point on the line $\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y+a}{1}=\frac{z}{1}=t_1 \quad$ (say) is $\left(t_1, t_1-a, t_1\right)$ and any point on the line $\frac{x+a}{2}=\frac{y}{1}=\frac{z}{1}=t_2 \quad($ say $)$ is $\left(2 t_2-a, t_2, t_2\right)$.
Now direction cosine of the lines intersecting the above lines is proportional to $\left(2 t_2-a-t_1, t_2-t_1+a, t_2-t_1\right)$.
Hence $2 \mathrm{t}_2-\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{t}_1=2 \mathrm{k}, \mathrm{t}_2-\mathrm{t}_1+\mathrm{a}=\mathrm{k}$ and $\mathrm{t}_2-\mathrm{t}_1=2 \mathrm{k}$
On solving these, we get $t_1=3 a, t_2=a$.
Hence points are $(3 a, 2 a, 3 a)$ and $(a, a, a)$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the area of the region bounded by the curves <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo></math>
question: If the area of the region bounded by the curves <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo></math>
solution:
Given,

Equations of curve y2-2y=-x and x+y=0

Now solving above equations we get, x=0,-3

Now plotting the area we get, 

Now from above diagram required area will be

A=∫032y-y2+ydy

⇒A=∫033y-y2dy

⇒A=3y22-y3303

⇒A=272-273

⇒A=276=92 sq. units

So, A=92⇒8A=9×4=36.

question:

$\mathrm{P}$ : Suman is brilliant
Q : Suman is rich
$\mathrm{R}$ : Suman is honest
The negation of the statement "Suman is brilliant and dishonest if and only if Suman is rich" can be expressed as
solution: $\sim\left\{\left(P_{\wedge} \sim R\right) \leftrightarrow Q\right\}=\sim\left\{Q \leftrightarrow\left(P_{\wedge} \sim R\right)\right\}$

subject: Mathematics
q: The integral <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∫</mo><mfrac><mrow><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>)</mo><mi>cos</mi><msqrt><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></msqrt></mrow><msqrt><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn></msqrt></mfrac><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></math> is equal to (where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> is a constant of integration)
question: The integral <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∫</mo><mfrac><mrow><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>)</mo><mi>cos</mi><msqrt><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></msqrt></mrow><msqrt><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn></msqrt></mfrac><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></math> is equal to (where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> is a constant of integration)
solution:
∫(2x-1)cos(2x-1)2+5(2x-1)2+5dx

(2x-1)2+5=t2

22x-1 2dx=2 tdt

2t2-5dx=tdt

So ∫t2-5cost2t2-5dt=12sint+c

=12sin(2x-1)2+5+c

question: Suppose <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo></math> be an arithmetic progression of natural numbers. If the ratio of the sum of the first five terms to the sum of first nine terms of the progression is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>17</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>110</mn><mo><</mo><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>15</mn></msub><mo><</mo><mn>120</mn></math>, then the sum of the first ten terms of the progression is equal to
solution:
Given the ratio of the sum of the first five terms to the sum of the first nine terms is 5:17,

So, S5S9=517⇒522a+4d922a+8d=517

⇒172a+4d=92a+8d

⇒34a+68d=18a+72d

⇒d=4a

Now a15=a+14d=57a

Also given 110<a15<120

⇒110<57a<120

⇒a=2  ∴ d=8

So, S10=1022×2+9×8=380
